Hamilton County is beyond grateful for this incredible team! Thank you for all you do!🙌 Hamilton County EMS
Our HCEMS Overdose Prevention Team expanded their mission a little today by helping prevent heat-related illnesses for our own crews.
 This afternoon, they surprised our field personnel with cold popsicles, catching up with them after calls at local emergency departments to help them cool down during this stretch of extreme heat.
 A big thank you to our Overdose Prevention Team for looking out for the well-being of those who are out serving Hamilton County every day. Stay cool, stay hydrated, and stay safe!

items.title: For two weeks in June, local high school students spent part of their summer break at UTC learning how the federal court...
items.content_text:
For two weeks in June, local high school students spent part of their summer break at UTC learning how the federal court system works. 👏 
 Twenty-five high schoolers participated in “The Supreme Court in My Hometown,” a national civics education program sponsored by the Supreme Court Historical Society, the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Sixth Circuit, the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Tennessee and the Adolph S. Ochs Professor of Government at UTC, Dr. Michelle D. Deardorff. 
 Participants heard presentations from judges, attorneys and professors, worked with UTC student mentors and visited the local courthouse, exploring the federal court system. 
 🔗  Read more here: https://blog.utc.edu/…/inside-the-judicial-branch-national…/

Hamilton County is cheering on Spain’s National Soccer Team as their FIFA World Cup journey continues. ⚽️🙏

 Thank you for making Hamilton County part of your World Cup journey. ¡Viva España! 🇪🇸

items.title: Congratulations to the first Early Childhood Neighborhood Council in Alton Park! Since December, this incredible group of parent,...
items.content_text:
Congratulations to the first Early Childhood Neighborhood Council in Alton Park!

Since December, this incredible group of parent, grandparent, and caregiver leaders has met to learn together, share their experiences, identify the strengths and needs of young children in Alton Park, and build the confidence and skills to advocate for families in their neighborhood.

This wasn't a parenting class. These resident leaders are helping shape how our community supports children before they ever enter kindergarten. Their ideas and lived experiences will help guide the work of more than 40 organizations that have committed to serving Alton Park more intentionally as we work toward becoming an Early Learning Community where every young child can thrive.

To this first Early Childhood Neighborhood Council: Thank you for your time, your honesty, your leadership, and your commitment to the youngest children in your neighborhood. The relationships you've built, the stories you've shared, and the leadership you've developed are already helping shape a stronger future for Alton Park's youngest children.

Special thanks to: the ELN Local Action Fund for investing in this pilot and helping make this work possible; to Jakita Blackmon for being the group's dedicated and trusted facilitator; to Erika Roberts (Velvet Poetry Productions) for supporting the Wisdom Lens project for the council; to Katherlyn L Geter for equipping the group with advocacy skills; and to the The Bethlehem Center for allowing us to host monthly meetings at the facility.

(Not pictured: LaTosha Tate, Brittany Strickland, and Jermiya Ballard.) 💙

items.content_text:
After 25 years of dedicated service, Captain Eric Stone is retiring.
 Throughout his career, he earned the respect of those around him through steady leadership, professionalism, and an unwavering commitment to the job. Whether mentoring younger firefighters or leading emergency responses, he set the standard for what it means to serve with integrity and compassion.
 His contributions to our department and community have left a lasting impact, and his leadership will be missed.
 Congratulations, Captain Stone, on your well-earned retirement! Thank you for your many years of service. We wish you all the best in the years ahead!

items.title: The OECD Survey on Drivers of Trust in Public Institutions 2026 Results is out now. Levels of trust in government have s...
items.content_text:
The OECD Survey on Drivers of Trust in Public Institutions 2026 Results is out now.
 Levels of trust in government have steadied since the survey began in 2021, with 40% of people expressing trust in their national government and signs of improvement in several countries. 
 68% of citizens believe voting works - an essential foundation of democracy - yet only 31% feel they have a real influence on what government does.
 This is one of several significant gaps revealed by the #OECDTrustSurvey, which explores the drivers of trust in public institutions and points to where reform is most needed.
 The analysis also shows that people trust the public services they count on every day, but are less confident about government’s ability to tackle long-term challenges.
 To protect and build trust, governments need to open up genuine opportunities to participate, increase transparency, ground decisions in evidence, and make sure new technology earns people’s confidence.
 The survey’s findings will be at the centre of discussions at the 2026 Global Forum on Building Trust and Reinforcing Democracy in October.
 Learn more about the Results: https://brnw.ch/21x3LLE

items.title: Official development assistance (ODA) for health could decline by up to 46% in 2026. A new #OECD policy brief projects t...
items.content_text:
Official development assistance (ODA) for health could decline by up to 46% in 2026.
 A new #OECD policy brief projects that health will face the deepest cuts among major development sectors, falling below pre-pandemic levels.
 ➡️ Population and reproductive health could see a decline of up to 54.1%
 ➡️ ODA for malaria control could fall by up to 59.6%
 ➡️ ODA for tuberculosis control could fall by up to 57.2%
 ➡️ ODA for other infectious-disease control could fall by up to 40.4%
 Health bears the deepest projected sectoral cuts, but it is not the only area affected. Humanitarian aid and support to government and civil society are also projected to decline significantly, by 40.3% and 39.8% respectively between 2024 and 2026.
 These reductions could affect funding for pandemic preparedness, prevention and response. OECD estimates suggest that ODA accounts for around 80% of financing for these activities in low-income countries, where the greatest risks often occur.
 The implications are particularly significant in countries facing disease outbreaks. The Democratic Republic of the Congo and Uganda are currently responding to Ebola outbreaks, while health ODA to both countries is projected to decline significantly.
 Read the policy brief: https://brnw.ch/21x3LBq
 #ODA2026 #OECDdevelopment

A job. A degree. A bachelor's pathway. All in one program.
 Chattanooga State, BlueCross BlueShield of Tennessee, and ETSU are creating even more opportunities for BlueHorizon apprentices.
 Students in BlueHorizon earn a Health Sciences degree from ChattState while working in paid positions at BlueCross BlueShield of Tennessee. Now, thanks to a new transfer agreement with ETSU, students can continue their education and earn a bachelor's degree in Professional Development.
 It's another way we're helping students gain real-world experience, earn a paycheck, and build a career with confidence.
 BlueHorizon will begin recruiting for its second cohort in August 2026, with classes starting in January 2027.

items.title: High school sports develops character and work ethic in student athletes and builds school pride for the entire student...
items.content_text:
High school sports develops character and work ethic in student athletes and builds school pride for the entire student body. The newest renderings of the new Brainerd Middle High School reflect our commitment to giving public school student athletes the tools and facilities to thrive. We released them at Brainerd Alumni events this weekend, which was a reminder of that the Panthers have one of the proudest alumni bases in our community.
 For too long, we underinvested in public school athletics, falling short of the expectations of students, parents, and coaches, and propelling private school athletic programs in the process. The school board is addressing this through increased coaching stipends, and so are we in county government with tens of millions being invested in new and improved public school athletic facilities. My goal is that when the next world class athlete like Ace Bailey comes along, he or she will have the confidence to follow in the tradition of generations of Brainerd Panthers who have succeeded in athletics, academics, and beyond their high school experience. 
 Brainerd High School

items.title: This morning we proposed a one year moratorium on data centers in Hamilton County. Frankly, I’m skeptical that they belo...
items.content_text:
This morning we proposed a one year moratorium on data centers in Hamilton County. Frankly, I’m skeptical that they belong in our rural communities, which are already seeing unprecedented residential development. This moratorium will give our new County Planning Department the opportunity to further study the impacts of data centers, engage our utility providers, and gather community feedback. 
 We have a responsibility to plan thoughtfully for the next generation and to preserve the beauty of our county.

items.content_text:
🎓✨ **Chattanooga Head Start Alumni Spotlight** ✨🎓 
 Chattanooga Head Start is proud to recognize Maurquez Thompson for his outstanding achievements and continued commitment to serving others.
 As the Co-Valedictorian of the Class of 2023 at Brainerd High School, Maurquez set an inspiring example of academic excellence, leadership, and determination. Today, as a rising senior at Stanford University, he continues to excel while remaining dedicated to giving back to the Chattanooga community that helped shape him.
 Maurquez is also a proud Chattanooga Head Start alumnus, and his journey is a powerful reminder of the lasting impact that Head Start can have on the lives of children and families. His success reflects what is possible when young children are provided with strong educational foundations, supportive relationships, and opportunities to thrive.
 We celebrate Maurquez not only for his academic accomplishments but also for his character, leadership, and commitment to uplifting others. He represents the promise, potential, and greatness that Head Start helps nurture every day.
 Congratulations, Maurquez! Your Chattanooga Head Start family is incredibly proud of you and look forward to seeing all that you will continue to accomplish.
 #ChattanoogaHeadStart #HeadStartWorks #HeadStartAlumni #FutureLeader #StanfordUniversity #BrainerdHighSchool #CommunityImpact #AcademicExcellence #GivingBack #ChattanoogaProud 🎉👏📚🌟

items.content_text:
The summer heat is here, and everyone is feeling its effects. To stay safe during extremely hot hours, it's important to recognize the signs of heat-related illnesses, such as cramps, dizziness, excessive sweating, and nausea.
 Certain groups are more vulnerable to the heat, including individuals working outdoors, older adults, pregnant women, young children, and those with chronic conditions. 
 To protect yourself, make sure to stay hydrated, wear lightweight clothing, and take breaks in the shade or air conditioning. Additionally, never leave children or pets in a parked car. 
 For more information on heat-related illnesses, please visit [CDC Heat Stress Information] https://www.cdc.gov/niosh/heat-stress/about/illnesses.html

items.title: Dangerous heat is expected across our area this week, with heat index values climbing well into the triple digits. High...
items.content_text:
Dangerous heat is expected across our area this week, with heat index values climbing well into the triple digits. High temperatures and humidity can quickly lead to heat-related illnesses.
 Stay hydrated by drinking plenty of water.
 Wear lightweight, light-colored clothing.
 Take frequent breaks in the shade or air conditioning.
 Never leave children or pets unattended in a vehicle—even for a moment.
 Check on elderly neighbors, family members, and anyone without reliable air conditioning.
 Signs of heat exhaustion include heavy sweating, weakness, dizziness, nausea, and headache. If someone becomes confused, loses consciousness, or stops sweating, call 911 immediately. These may be signs of life-threatening heat stroke.
 Hamilton County EMS encourages everyone to take this heat seriously. A few simple precautions can help keep you and your loved ones safe.

items.title: Congratulations to the Leadership Chattanooga Class of 2026! 🎓 Over the past nine months, this remarkable group of leaders has cha...
items.content_text:
Congratulations to the Leadership Chattanooga Class of 2026! 🎓  

Over the past nine months, this remarkable group of leaders has challenged themselves, built meaningful connections, and deepened their commitment to our community. We are proud to celebrate their accomplishments and the impact they will continue to make across the Chattanooga region.  

A special thank you to Class Representatives Rosie Kehinde and Ken Jones for delivering thoughtful and inspiring remarks on behalf of the class at graduation.  We are also grateful to our program sponsor, Tennessee Valley Authority, for their continued investment in leadership development, and to EPB for helping make our graduation celebration extra special.  

Here's to the Leadership Chattanooga Class of 2026 and all that's ahead!

 Session 1: 12:00 p.m. – 1:00 p.m.

 Located at The Chattanooga Public Library (downtown)
 Learn about the proposed changes, ask questions, and share your feedback with our team. Your input plays an important role in helping us improve service for our riders and community.
 We hope to see you there! 🚌
 Learn more by visiting www.gocarta.org.

items.content_text:
Chattanooga! Outdoor temperatures may become dangerously high this week. Take extra care to stay hydrated. If it gets too hot, move indoors and don’t forget to bring your pets! 
 - Keep blinds and curtains closed to block out the sun
 - Use fans to help circulate air (set ceiling fans to spin counterclockwise)
 - Raise your thermostat a few degrees when you're not home
 - Skip the oven and dryer during peak heat hours
 - Check for air leaks around windows and doors
 Small changes can make a big impact!

items.title: ArtsBuild Launches Arts Voter Guide for Hamilton County Elections - Check it Out at the Link Below! CHATTANOOGA, TN
items.content_text:
ArtsBuild Launches Arts Voter Guide for Hamilton County Elections - Check it Out at the Link Below!
 CHATTANOOGA, TN – ArtsBuild has released its 2026 Arts Voter Guide, a nonpartisan resource highlighting where Hamilton County candidates stand on arts and culture. The guide features responses from candidates for Hamilton County Mayor, County Commission, and School Board.
 With early voting beginning July 17 and Election Day on August 6, ArtsBuild encourages voters to learn where candidates stand on issues affecting the arts. Hamilton County's nonprofit arts and culture sector employs more than 3,000 people and generates nearly $200 million in annual economic activity while enriching education, strengthening neighborhoods, and expanding access to creative opportunities.
 "The Arts Voter Guide is a resource for every Hamilton County voter," said ArtsBuild President James McKissic. "Our goal is to provide voters with the information they need to make informed decisions and better understand each candidate's perspective on the role of the arts in our community."
 As part of its mission to build stronger communities through the arts, ArtsBuild supports the nonprofit arts sector and advocates for arts education and access throughout Chattanooga and Hamilton County. The Arts Voter Guide is designed to help voters make informed decisions while remaining strictly nonpartisan.
 The guide is available now at artsbuild.com/advocacy.
 About ArtsBuild
 ArtsBuild is a Chattanooga-based 501(c)(3) nonprofit dedicated to building stronger communities through the arts. Since 1969, ArtsBuild has invested more than $80 million in Hamilton County through grants to arts organizations, arts education programs for students and teachers, and initiatives that expand access to the arts for all residents.  Support our work at: https://secure.qgiv.com/for/aaogc/

This week marks one year since Dr. Lori Bruce began her role as Chancellor of the University of Tennessee at Chattanooga.
 Over the past year, she has met with students, faculty, staff, alumni, and community leaders across Chattanooga and beyond. Along the way, she discovered something that continues to shape how she thinks about UTC:
 "It isn't a University that simply resides in Chattanooga. It is Chattanooga."
 As Chancellor Bruce begins her second year, we're looking back on the experiences, lessons, and people that made year one memorable—and looking ahead to all that's still to come.
 Read the full Q&A: https://blog.utc.edu/…/one-year-in-chancellor-lori-bruce-r…/

items.title: 🗳️ Planning to show your support this election season?   Before you plant that campaign sign, we want to make sure you...
items.content_text:
🗳️ Planning to show your support this election season?  
 Before you plant that campaign sign, we want to make sure you know the ground rules.  
 To keep roadways safe and protect driver visibility, yard signs need to be back from the road.  Signs up to 10 square feet should be 5 feet off of the back of curb and signs up to 32 square feet need to be 10 feet back.  At roadway intersections where cars are making turns, signs cannot be within 25 feet of the curb.  The no-sign zones are shown in the graphic.  
 Signs are never allowed in medians or traffic islands and they cannot be attached to trees, sign posts, fences or utility poles.  
 Campaigns must register a contact person with the Building Official at City Hall prior to posting signs.  For more information, call (423) 867-7711.  
 Thanks for being engaged in the democratic process and for your help in keeping campaign season safe for everyone here on the Ridge !

items.content_text:
HCSO Deputies Apprehend Wanted Man with Help of Drone Technology
 Hamilton County, TN – On June 28, 2026, HCSO deputies located a suspect with multiple felony warrants on Shahan Lane in Meigs County.
 A drone was deployed over the wooded area where deputies believed the suspect, Thurman Massengale, was hiding, and drone technology was used to direct the deputies on foot to Massengale. During the search, a man with active warrants out of Meigs County was also taken into custody.
 We want to thank the Hamilton County Office of Emergency Management and the Meigs County Sheriff’s Office for their help during this incident.
 This information is preliminary and subject to change. No further details are available.

items.title: Our Final Destination: Passport to Art 🎨 Passport to Art took students on a creative journey around the world! Each day...
items.content_text:
Our Final Destination: Passport to Art 🎨 

Passport to Art took students on a creative journey around the world! Each day, campers explored a different country by learning about its culture, landmarks, and traditions before creating an art project inspired by what they learned. They visited Brazil, where they drew colorful toucans; Japan, where they painted beautiful cherry blossoms; Egypt, where they illustrated the famous pyramids; and Ghana, where they designed vibrant Kente cloth patterns. This camp combined geography, culture, and creativity while introducing young artists to traditions from around the world.

That's a wrap on our Creative Camp Spotlights! We hope everyone had an amazing time at Summer Reach. Enjoy the rest of your summer, and we'll see you back at school soon!

items.title: Please join us in welcoming Emma Dunavin, MPH to our SETD and Southeast Tennessee Area Agency on Aging and Disability (SETAAAD) te...
items.content_text:
Please join us in welcoming Emma Dunavin, MPH to our SETD and Southeast Tennessee Area Agency on Aging and Disability (SETAAAD) team as our new Quality Assurance Coordinator for SETAAAD! 

A Chattanooga native, Emma recently returned home after nearly three years in St. Louis, where she worked in public health supporting HIV prevention and harm reduction initiatives. She earned both her bachelor's and Master of Public Health degrees from the University of Tennessee, Knoxville, and brings valuable experience in community health, policy, and public service.

Emma is excited to be back in Chattanooga with her husband as they put down roots, and we're excited to have her helping serve the communities of Southeast Tennessee. Welcome to the team, Emma!

items.title: Our Fire Academy cadets recently completed intensive Rapid Intervention Team (RIT) training to develop the hands-on skil...
items.content_text:
Our Fire Academy cadets recently completed intensive Rapid Intervention Team (RIT) training to develop the hands-on skills needed to rescue firefighters in distress. The rigorous week covered critical survival techniques, including victim location, air management, packaging and removal of a down firefighter, and coordinated rescue operations.
 These scenarios challenged cadets physically and mentally, proving that successful operations depend on preparation, discipline, and teamwork.
 We sincerely thank our instructors for their passion, commitment, and expertise in preparing our future firefighters to serve our community and each other!

items.title: ArtsBuild Launches Arts Voter Guide for Hamilton County Elections - Check it Out at the Link Below! CHATTANOOGA, TN
items.content_text:
ArtsBuild Launches Arts Voter Guide for Hamilton County Elections - Check it Out at the Link Below!
 CHATTANOOGA, TN – ArtsBuild has released its 2026 Arts Voter Guide, a nonpartisan resource highlighting where Hamilton County candidates stand on arts and culture. The guide features responses from candidates for Hamilton County Mayor, County Commission, and School Board.
 With early voting beginning July 17 and Election Day on August 6, ArtsBuild encourages voters to learn where candidates stand on issues affecting the arts. Hamilton County's nonprofit arts and culture sector employs more than 3,000 people and generates nearly $200 million in annual economic activity while enriching education, strengthening neighborhoods, and expanding access to creative opportunities.
 "The Arts Voter Guide is a resource for every Hamilton County voter," said ArtsBuild President James McKissic. "Our goal is to provide voters with the information they need to make informed decisions and better understand each candidate's perspective on the role of the arts in our community."
 As part of its mission to build stronger communities through the arts, ArtsBuild supports the nonprofit arts sector and advocates for arts education and access throughout Chattanooga and Hamilton County. The Arts Voter Guide is designed to help voters make informed decisions while remaining strictly nonpartisan.
 The guide is available now at artsbuild.com/advocacy.
 About ArtsBuild
 ArtsBuild is a Chattanooga-based 501(c)(3) nonprofit dedicated to building stronger communities through the arts. Since 1969, ArtsBuild has invested more than $80 million in Hamilton County through grants to arts organizations, arts education programs for students and teachers, and initiatives that expand access to the arts for all residents.  Support our work at: https://secure.qgiv.com/for/aaogc/

items.title: Great group of volunteers from TVFCU out at Citico Creek today!  We are fighting the never ending litter problem. We kno...
items.content_text:
Great group of volunteers from TVFCU out at Citico Creek today! 
 We are fighting the never ending litter problem. We know we will never be perfect, but as a community, we have to do better.
CLEANING UP AT CITICO CREEK: 
 I’m here at Citico Creek in Orchard Knob picking up litter with TVFCU! 
 We have found so much litter here! Most of this litter wasn’t dropped here at the creek but on the streets around Chattanooga. Everytime it rains that litter will end up here and then eventually the Tennessee River. 
 You can do your part by picking up all the litter you can! And first and foremost not litter in the first place!

items.title: 40% of people have confidence in their national government, according to the latest #OECDTrustData survey. Covering 33 #...
items.content_text:
40% of people have confidence in their national government, according to the latest #OECDTrustData survey.
 Covering 33 #OECD countries and five accession candidates, the survey helps governments track trust levels across institutions and pinpoints where reform is needed most.
 What do people say across OECD?
 ➡️ 40% trust national government
 ➡️ 46% trust local government
 ➡️ 42% believe decisions are evidence based
 ➡️ 68% believe voting makes a difference
 ➡️ 31% feel people like them have a real say in what government does.
 ➡️ 41% see potential in government use of AI
 The OECD Survey on Drivers of Trust in Public Institutions 2026 Results points to clear priorities for action: strengthening decision making on complex issues, fostering meaningful citizen participation, using technologies transparently and focusing on what drives trust in each institution.
 Find out more: https://brnw.ch/21x3JIm
 #BetterPoliciesForBetterLives

items.title: A new chapter is beginning for one of Hamilton County’s most significant landscapes. Tennessee State Parks is inviting t...
items.content_text:
A new chapter is beginning for one of Hamilton County’s most significant landscapes.
 Tennessee State Parks is inviting the public to help shape the future of a new state park at McDonald Farm. Approximately 1,300 acres of the farm will be transferred to the State of Tennessee, creating a new park that will eventually encompass about 2,500 acres and connect to the Cumberland Trail.
 This is an incredible opportunity to preserve a special place for future generations while creating new opportunities for recreation, conservation, and connection to our region’s history and natural resources.
 Community input will help guide the park’s master plan. Attend the open house on July 16 from 5–7 p.m. at the Harvest Barn at McDonald Farm (register below) or share your thoughts through the online survey: https://www.surveymonkey.com/r/TSP70PublicLink
 Register to attend via the the State of TN website: https://www.tn.gov/…/2…/6/26/tnsp-public-input-new-park.html #tennesseestateparks

items.title: Although wildlife may appear cute, feeding them can be dangerous for both you and the animals. Animal bites are a common...
items.content_text:
Although wildlife may appear cute, feeding them can be dangerous for both you and the animals. Animal bites are a common source of serious infections and can occur through contact with an infected animal, a contaminated surface, or its habitat.
 Bites and scratches pose a significant risk of illness, including rabies, which primarily spreads through bites or scratches from infected wild animals such as raccoons, skunks, or foxes.
 To protect yourself, your family, and your pets, it's best to keep a safe distance from wildlife. For more information, please visit health.hamiltontn.org/…/se…/environmental/rabiescontrol.aspx or call (423) 209-8110.

it’s important to remember that even after a storm moves through, hidden dangers can remain... especially around downed power lines.
 Always assume a line is energized and keep your distance. Stay at least 30 feet away, keep children and pets back, and report the hazard to EPB right away.
 Our crews are trained and ready to respond safely. Your job is simple: stay back and stay safe!

items.title: Incomes for farmers and other agricultural workers are projected to rise over the next decade, but progress will remain...
items.content_text:
Incomes for farmers and other agricultural workers are projected to rise over the next decade, but progress will remain uneven.
 While average global income per worker is projected to grow by 9% over the next decade, farmers in low-income regions of Sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia still earn very little, with only modest gains ahead.
 Limited access to machinery, markets and higher-value products keeps agriculture worker incomes low. Boosting productivity, improving market access, and creating more rural job opportunities will be key to closing the gap and strengthening food security.
 Learn more about the state of agriculture today in latest #OECD-FAO Agricultural Outlook, launched today by OECD Secretary-General Mathias Cormann and FAO Director-General QU Dongyu: https://brnw.ch/21x3Jky
 #AgOutlook

items.title: We’re excited to introduce the 16 poets selected for Democracy Speaks, Chattanooga’s new civic poetry pathway at Renaiss...
items.content_text:
We’re excited to introduce the 16 poets selected for Democracy Speaks, Chattanooga’s new civic poetry pathway at Renaissance Park.
 Through a community selection process, these poets were chosen to create original works exploring democracy, history, civic engagement, and the many voices that make up our community. We are also proud that every poet participating in this project was compensated for their creative contributions.
 Selected poets and poems:
 • Amber Hickey – River Cache
 • Cheikh “CJ” Diakhate – Burdens
 • Cody Simon – Middle Ground
 • Desiree’ “Dezigner” Green – We Shall Overcome
 • Kelly “Stormy Poet” Williams – Chattanooga National Cemetery Speaks
 • Mirabelle Soufer – How Long
 • Philip Luckey – Garden Variety Democracy
 • Vander – Lacked Intolerance
 • Peggy Douglas – Abby Crawford Milton
 • Sylvia J. Greene – Eleanor and Mary
 • Kweens Crown – Rooted In Zion
 • Marcus Lee – Just A Song
 • Naomi Setliffe – Nineteenth is a Doorway
 • N’nako Kande’ – The Land Remembers
 • R. N. Letterman – Garden of Eden: The Watauga Longhunters and the Battle of Kings Mountain
 • Kenneth Allen Basham, III – America: A Nation of Mistaken Identity
 Thank you to our incredible partners at Rhyme N Chatt, who brought this vision to us and helped shape it into a meaningful public art experience. Special thanks to Project Manager Marsha Mills and community mentors and reviewers Laura Brown, Ian Seaton, and Linda Moss-Mines for their guidance, expertise, and thoughtful participation throughout the process.
 Join us on June 28 at Renaissance Park as we celebrate the opening of Democracy Speaks and hear these powerful voices come to life through poetry, community, and conversation.
 This Tennessee America 250 Program is supported in part by the Tennessee Commission for the United States Semiquincentennial.

items.title: Chattanooga Fire worked a motor vehicle crash with injuries and possible entrapment Saturday night on Hixson Pike at N A...
items.content_text:
Chattanooga Fire worked a motor vehicle crash with injuries and possible entrapment Saturday night on Hixson Pike at N Access Road involving two vehicles and multiple patients. 
 One vehicle was flipped on its top with one person trapped inside, unable to get out. There were a total of four passengers. The second vehicle had all parties out of the vehicle upon arrival. Firefighters worked together to free the victim from the wreckage. Once the patient was removed from the car, they were immediately placed in the ambulance for patient care. In total, three patients were transported to the hospital by HCEMS. CFD personnel cleaned up debris and placed oil dry where needed before returning to service.
 Engine 16, Squad 19, Squad 7 and Battalion 3 Red Shift responded. Photos and details from Captain Michael Battle. Crash investigations are conducted by CPD.

items.content_text:
In Asia, just 15% of listed companies disclose human capital information, such as employee turnover, health and safety training, workplace accidents, compared with 22% globally.
 Human capital management is becoming increasingly important, reflecting growing recognition of workforce‑related risks and opportunities.
 With investor and policymakers paying greater attention to issues such as talent, skills and employee well-being, how can countries improve reporting practices?
 Read the #OECDAsiaCapitalMarkets report to find out ➡️ https://brnw.ch/21x3Ib5
 #BetterPoliciesForBetterLives

items.title: Official development assistance (ODA) is projected to decline by 6.9% in 2026.  A new #OECD policy brief projects that n...
items.content_text:
Official development assistance (ODA) is projected to decline by 6.9% in 2026. 
 A new #OECD policy brief projects that net ODA could fall to its lowest level since 2014, marking a third consecutive year of decline. 
 Most of the expected reductions in 2026 come from a small number of the largest providers, although cuts are expected to be widespread across Development Assistance Committee (DAC) members. 
 The policy brief examines the implications for vulnerable countries and sectors, including: 
 ➡️ Further declines in bilateral ODA to sub-Saharan Africa and least developed countries 
 ➡️ Deep cuts to health, humanitarian aid and support to government and civil society 
 ➡️ A third consecutive year of decline in multilateral ODA, including reduced core funding to United Nations organisations 
 The policy brief suggests that the factors behind recent aid cuts are likely to persist, including fiscal consolidation, geopolitical tensions, increased spending on defence and security, and efforts to align public spending with domestic priorities. At the same time, needs in developing countries continue to grow. 
 Read the policy brief ➡️ https://brnw.ch/21x3HPX
 #ODA2026 #OECDdevelopment

items.content_text:
Shooting – Highway 153 South between Thrasher Bridge and Exit 5A
 The Chattanooga Police Department (CPD) is investigating a shooting that was reported to have occurred on Highway 153 South between Thrasher Bridge and Exit 5A. 
 At 4:40 A.M., CPD officers responded to reports of a person shot in the 4900 block of Bonny Oaks Drive. Upon arrival, officers learned the incident had not occurred at that location. The victim, a 15-year-old male, was taken to a local hospital by Hamilton County EMS with non-life-threatening injuries. 
 CPD Homicide and Crime Scene Units responded to investigate and canvas the area of the reported shooting location. The preliminary investigation indicates the victim was a passenger in a vehicle that was involved in a road rage incident with the suspect's vehicle. The investigation is ongoing and all leads are being thoroughly pursued. 
 This information is preliminary and subject to change as the investigation continues. Chattanooga Police ask anyone with information regarding this incident to call 423-698-2525.
 #CPDnews

Shooting – Highway 153 South between Thrasher Bridge and Exit 5A
The Chattanooga Police Department (CPD) is investigating a shooting that was reported to have occurred on Highway 153 South between Thrasher Bridge and Exit 5A.
At 4:40 A.M., CPD officers responded to reports of a person shot in the 4900 block of Bonny Oaks Drive. Upon arrival, officers learned the incident had not occurred at that location. The victim, a 15-year-old male, was taken to a local hospital by Hamilton County EMS with non-life-threatening injuries.
CPD Homicide and Crime Scene Units responded to investigate and canvas the area of the reported shooting location. The preliminary investigation indicates the victim was a passenger in a vehicle that was involved in a road rage incident with the suspect's vehicle. The investigation is ongoing and all leads are being thoroughly pursued.
This information is preliminary and subject to change as the investigation continues. Chattanooga Police ask anyone with information regarding this incident to call 423-698-2525.
#CPDnews

items.title: Asian capital markets are growing but companies are not being valued much higher. Across the region, nearly 4 out of 10...
items.content_text:
Asian capital markets are growing but companies are not being valued much higher.
 Across the region, nearly 4 out of 10 companies in 2025 were valued by investors at less than what they’re worth on paper - much higher than in the rest of the world.
 Why is this happening and what can governments do to help companies use money more effectively, increase shareholder returns and make markets more competitive?
 Find out in the #OECDAsiaCapitalMarkets Report ➡️ https://brnw.ch/21x3HaY
 #BetterPoliciesForBetterLives

items.title: What should I do if ICE stops me in my car?🤔🚨 We share our Know Your Rights with YOU video series...
items.content_text:
What should I do if ICE stops me in my car?🤔🚨
 We share our Know Your Rights video series with TIRRC. In this video we inform you about the rights you have if you are detained by ICE in your vehicle.
 Please stay calm, evaluate your situation and offer ONLY valid information. For more information and educational videos, visit our Resources for Immigrant Families page here -> https://www.lapazchattanooga.org/recursos 
 @followers
In this episode of Know Your Rights with Viri Marín from the Tennessee Immigrant and Refugee Rights Coalition [TIRRC] we talk about what we can do when an ICE agent stops us in our vehicle.
 In this video, we talk about the rights you have when you are stopped in your car and how important it is to evaluate and understand the situation you are in. Please be sure to save your car information and provide only valid information. 🌿

items.title: Today marks National PTSD Awareness Day, so let's talk about this devastating mental health condition. Did you know that...
items.content_text:
Today marks National PTSD Awareness Day, so let's talk about this devastating mental health condition.
 Did you know that about 6% of U.S. adults will experience PTSD at some point in their lives? The rate is 10 -20% for first responders due to their repeated exposure to traumatic events throughout their careers. The good news is that a variety of treatment options are available. But first, you must recognize the symptoms. They include flashbacks, sleep disturbances, hypervigilance, irritability, emotional numbness, and difficulty concentrating.
 Did you know that about 6% of U.S. adults will experience PTSD at some point in their lives? That rate is significantly higher for first responders, who are repeatedly exposed to traumatic events throughout their careers. Studies estimate that 10-20% of first responders may experience PTSD symptoms during their careers. The good news is that a variety of treatment options are available. But first, you must recognize the symptoms. They include flashbacks, sleep disturbances, hypervigilance, irritability, emotional numbness, and difficulty concentrating.
 CPD has prioritized employee wellbeing by creating a dedicated Wellness Unit. It includes a proactive Peer Support Program that launched in 2016, a Peer Support Mentor for cadets, wellness training focused on resilience, stress management, and trauma awareness, resources for employees and their families, and efforts to reduce stigma around mental health.
 Remember, if you or a loved one are in crisis you can call or text 988 anytime.

items.title: Official development assistance (ODA) is projected to decline by 6.9% in 2026.  A new #OECD policy brief projects that n...
items.content_text:
Official development assistance (ODA) is projected to decline by 6.9% in 2026. 
 A new #OECD policy brief projects that net ODA could fall to its lowest level since 2014, marking a third consecutive year of decline. 
 Most of the expected reductions in 2026 come from a small number of the largest providers, although cuts are expected to be widespread across Development Assistance Committee (DAC) members. 
 The policy brief examines the implications for vulnerable countries and sectors, including: 
 ➡️ Further declines in bilateral ODA to sub-Saharan Africa and least developed countries 
 ➡️ Deep cuts to health, humanitarian aid and support to government and civil society 
 ➡️ A third consecutive year of decline in multilateral ODA, including reduced core funding to United Nations organisations 
 The policy brief suggests that the factors behind recent aid cuts are likely to persist, including fiscal consolidation, geopolitical tensions, increased spending on defence and security, and efforts to align public spending with domestic priorities. At the same time, needs in developing countries continue to grow. 
 Read the policy brief: https://brnw.ch/21x3GMU
 #ODA2026 #OECDdevelopment
